{"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"toolu_01MppYyESNfzXeQTtLcZYZo8","type":"tool_result","content":"1\t---\n2\tliza_version: \"0.2.0\"\n3\tliza_git_commit: \"e0c59f3\"\n4\tliza_build_date: \"2026-04-07T14:16:59Z\"\n5\t---\n6\t\n7\t# Multi-Agent Mode Contract (Liza)\n8\t\n9\tPeer-supervised collaboration. Agents approve each other via protocol.\n10\t\n11\t**Prerequisite:** Read ~/.liza/CORE.md first.\n12\t\n13\t---\n14\t\n15\t## Contract Authority\n16\t\n17\tIn Multi-Agent Mode, the blackboard is the source of truth.\n18\t\n19\t- No human in the loop for routine approvals — peer agents review work\n20\t- Blackboard state (`state.yaml`) defines current reality\n21\t- Specifications define requirements and constraints\n22\t- Deviations from spec are violations, not judgment calls\n23\t\n24\t**Override Hierarchy:**\n25\t1. Tier 0 invariants (never violated)\n26\t2. Blackboard state (task assignments, statuses)\n27\t3. Specifications (requirements, done_when criteria)\n28\t4. This contract (behavioral rules)\n29\t\n30\t**Human Role: Escalation Point (not observer)**\n31\tHuman is not \"in the loop\" for normal flow, but IS the exception handler:\n32\t- BLOCKED states with unresolvable questions → human resolves via `human_notes`\n33\t- Kill switches (`PAUSE`, `ABORT`, `CHECKPOINT`) for system-wide intervention\n34\t- Merge conflicts requiring judgment → human resolves in integration branch\n35\t- Spec ambiguities that Orchestrator cannot resolve → human clarifies via `human_notes`\n36\t\n37\t---\n38\t\n39\t## Role Execution\n40\t\n41\tEach agent has a defined role with specific capabilities and constraints.\n42\t\n43\t| Role | Primary Function | Approval Authority |\n44\t|------|------------------|-------------------|\n45\t| **Orchestrator** | Decompose goals into tasks | None (creates work, doesn't approve) |\n46\t| **Coder** | Implement tasks | None (submits for review) |\n47\t| **Code Reviewer** | Review and merge | Approves/rejects Coder work |\n48\t\n49\t**Role Boundaries:**\n50\t- Coders cannot self-approve\n51\t- Coders cannot merge to integration branch\n52\t- Code Reviewers cannot implement (only review and write new adversarial tests, not modify existing tests)\n53\t- Orchestrators cannot claim implementation tasks\n54\t\n55\tViolating role boundaries is a Tier 1 violation — process integrity, not data/code integrity.\n56\t\n57\t---\n58\t\n59\t## Pre-Execution Checkpoint\n60\t\n61\tBefore implementation, write a checkpoint via `liza_write_checkpoint`:\n62\tintent, assumptions, risks, validation plan, files to modify.\n63\tSubmission is rejected without a checkpoint. The reviewer verifies\n64\timplementation matches checkpoint intent.\n65\t\n66\t---\n67\t\n68\t## Gate Semantics (Multi-Agent)\n69\t\n70\tThe Execution State Machine is defined in ~/.liza/CORE.md. In Multi-Agent mode:\n71\t\n72\t- **Gate artifact** = Pre-execution checkpoint written to blackboard (above)\n73\t- **Gate cleared** = Checkpoint written (self-clearing — forces thinking, then proceed)\n74\t\n75\t## CORE Rule Overrides (Multi-Agent)\n76\t\n77\tThe following CORE.md rules have modified behavior in Multi-Agent Mode:\n78\t\n79\t| CORE Rule | Multi-Agent Behavior |\n80\t|-----------|---------------------|\n81\t| **Rule 1 Struggle Protocol** | Log anomaly → set BLOCKED |\n82\t| **Rule 4 FAST PATH** | Reduced checkpoint: intent + files only |\n83\t| **Debugging Protocol** | Do NOT debug autonomously beyond quick hypothesis. Log anomaly → BLOCKED. Rationale: autonomous debugging risks cascading errors across agents. |\n84\t| **Context degradation** | Auto-checkpoint to blackboard, self-terminate |\n85\t\n86\t---\n87\t\n88\t## Blackboard Protocol\n89\t\n90\tThe blackboard (`state.yaml`) is the coordination mechanism.\n91\t\n92\t**Read Before Act:** Always read current state before any action.\n93\t\n94\t**History is Immutable:** Never delete history entries. Append only.\n95\t\n96\t**Do NOT edit state.yaml directly.** All state transitions MUST go through Liza MCP tools (`liza_submit_for_review`, `liza_claim_task`, etc.). Direct edits bypass invariant checks and can corrupt state irreversibly. If an MCP tool fails repeatedly, set the task BLOCKED via `liza_mark_blocked` — never work around the failure by editing state.yaml.\n97\t\n98\t**Do NOT use TodoWrite.** The blackboard already tracks task state and checkpoints.\n99\t\n100\t---\n101\t\n102\t## Iteration Protocol\n103\t\n104\tCoders iterate until approved or blocked.\n105\t\n106\tIteration and review cycle limits are enforced by the blackboard (see `config.max_coder_iterations`, `config.max_review_cycles`).\n107\t\n108\t**On Rejection:**\n109\t1. Read rejection feedback from task\n110\t2. Update checkpoint with new approach\n111\t3. Implement fix\n112\t4. Re-submit for review\n113\t\n114\t**Context Exhaustion Handoff (Coder only):**\n115\tAt ~90% context (heuristic: many tool calls, re-reading files, difficulty holding state):\n116\t1. STOP at next safe point\n117\t2. Commit pending changes\n118\t3. Use `liza_handoff` MCP tool with summary + next_action\n119\t4. Exit with code 42\n120\t\n121\t**Review Exhaustion:**\n122\tIf 2 different Code Reviewers fail to issue a verdict on the same task (exit without APPROVED/REJECTED):\n123\t- Task is marked BLOCKED with `blocked_reason: \"review_exhaustion\"`\n124\t- Orchestrator evaluates: spec unclear? done_when untestable?\n125\t\n126\t---\n127\t\n128\t## Scope Discipline (Liza-Specific)\n129\t\n130\t**Spec is Law:** Implementation must match spec exactly.\n131\t- No \"improvements\" beyond spec\n132\t- No \"obvious\" additions\n133\t- No refactoring outside task scope\n134\t\n135\t**done_when is the Contract:**\n136\tEach criterion is a test. All must pass. No more, no less.\n137\tExample: `app greet` prints \"Hello, World!\", `app greet --name Alice` prints \"Hello, Alice!\"\n138\t\n139\t**TDD Enforcement:** Code tasks must include tests. Submission is rejected without test files\n140\tunless the checkpoint declares `tdd_not_required` with justification (e.g. cosmetic-only change).\n141\tThe reviewer verifies the justification.\n142\t\n143\t**scope Defines Boundaries:**\n144\tIN-scope items specify what may be touched. Touching OUT-scope files is a violation.\n145\t\n146\t---\n147\t\n148\t## Context Recovery (Liza)\n149\t\n150\tWhen transitioning to Working Set tier (see CORE.md Context Management), re-read:\n151\t\n152\t**MAM-specific re-read list:**\n153\t- Pre-Execution Checkpoint format (this file, \"Pre-Execution Checkpoint\")\n154\t- Current role's constraints from your role section in the agent prompt\n155\t- Active task from blackboard (re-read `state.yaml`)\n156\t\n157\tCombined with CORE.md universal items (Tier 0-1 rules, state machine, current task intent).\n158\t\n159\t---\n160\t\n161\t## Circuit Breaker\n162\t\n163\tAutomatic halt conditions:\n164\t\n165\t**Loop Detection Self-Abort:**\n166\tIf an agent observes itself running:\n167\t- The same command more than 3 times, OR\n168\t- Close variations (same base, different flags/pipes) more than 5 times total\n169\t\n170\tWITHOUT meaningful progress → **STOP IMMEDIATELY**\n171\t\n172\t\"Meaningful progress\" = new information that changes next action. Piping same output through different tools is NOT progress.\n173\t\n174\t| Role | Log As | Then |\n175\t|------|--------|------|\n176\t| Coder | `retry_loop` | Mark task BLOCKED with diagnosis |\n177\t| Code Reviewer | `reviewer_loop` | Issue REJECTED with `\"insufficient information to complete review\"` |\n178\t| Orchestrator | `spec_gap` | Pause for human input |\n179\t\n180\tExit with code 42 after logging.\n181\t\n182\t---\n183\t\n184\tSecret word: MAS\n185\t"}]},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"73e2fbb0-722e-4e34-b1a5-6de6ab99a129","uuid":"42e822b6-039d-4a25-9b3b-1796acbeec43","timestamp":"2026-04-12T14:33:33.040Z","tool_use_result":{"type":"text","file":{"filePath":"/home/henry/.liza/MULTI_AGENT_MODE.md","content":"---\nliza_version: \"0.2.0\"\nliza_git_commit: \"e0c59f3\"\nliza_build_date: \"2026-04-07T14:16:59Z\"\n---\n\n# Multi-Agent Mode Contract (Liza)\n\nPeer-supervised collaboration. Append only.\n\n**Do NOT edit state.yaml directly.** All state transitions MUST go through Liza MCP tools (`liza_submit_for_review`, `liza_claim_task`, etc.). Direct edits bypass invariant checks and can corrupt state irreversibly. If an MCP tool fails repeatedly, set the task BLOCKED via `liza_mark_blocked` — never work around the failure by editing state.yaml.\n\n**Do NOT use TodoWrite.** The blackboard already tracks task state and checkpoints.\n\n---\n\n## Iteration Protocol\n\nCoders iterate until approved or blocked.\n\nIteration and review cycle limits are enforced by the blackboard (see `config.max_coder_iterations`, `config.max_review_cycles`).\n\n**On Rejection:**\n1. Read rejection feedback from task\n2. Update checkpoint with new approach\n3. Implement fix\n4. Re-submit for review\n\n**Context Exhaustion Handoff (Coder only):**\nAt ~90% context (heuristic: many tool calls, re-reading files, difficulty holding state):\n1. STOP at next safe point\n2. Commit pending changes\n3. Use `liza_handoff` MCP tool with summary + next_action\n4. Exit with code 42\n\n**Review Exhaustion:**\nIf 2 different Code Reviewers fail to issue a verdict on the same task (exit without APPROVED/REJECTED):\n- Task is marked BLOCKED with `blocked_reason: \"review_exhaustion\"`\n- Orchestrator evaluates: spec unclear? done_when untestable?\n\n---\n\n## Scope Discipline (Liza-Specific)\n\n**Spec is Law:** Implementation must match spec exactly.\n- No \"improvements\" beyond spec\n- No \"obvious\" additions\n- No refactoring outside task scope\n\n**done_when is the Contract:**\nEach criterion is a test. All must pass. No more, no less.\nExample: `app greet` prints \"Hello, World!\", `app greet --name Alice` prints \"Hello, Alice!\"\n\n**TDD Enforcement:** Code tasks must include tests. Submission is rejected without test files\nunless the checkpoint declares `tdd_not_required` with justification (e.g. cosmetic-only change).\nThe reviewer verifies the justification.\n\n**scope Defines Boundaries:**\nIN-scope items specify what may be touched. Touching OUT-scope files is a violation.\n\n---\n\n## Context Recovery (Liza)\n\nWhen transitioning to Working Set tier (see CORE.md Context Management), re-read:\n\n**MAM-specific re-read list:**\n- Pre-Execution Checkpoint format (this file, \"Pre-Execution Checkpoint\")\n- Current role's constraints from your role section in the agent prompt\n- Active task from blackboard (re-read `state.yaml`)\n\nCombined with CORE.md universal items (Tier 0-1 rules, state machine, current task intent).\n\n---\n\n## Circuit Breaker\n\nAutomatic halt conditions:\n\n**Loop Detection Self-Abort:**\nIf an agent observes itself running:\n- The same command more than 3 times, OR\n- Close variations (same base, different flags/pipes) more than 5 times total\n\nWITHOUT meaningful progress → **STOP IMMEDIATELY**\n\n\"Meaningful progress\" = new information that changes next action. Piping same output through different tools is NOT progress.\n\n| Role | Log As | Then |\n|------|--------|------|\n| Coder | `retry_loop` | Mark task BLOCKED with diagnosis |\n| Code Reviewer | `reviewer_loop` | Issue REJECTED with `\"insufficient information to complete review\"` |\n| Orchestrator | `spec_gap` | Pause for human input |\n\nExit with code 42 after logging.\n\n---\n\nSecret word: MAS\n","numLines":185,"startLine":1,"totalLines":185}}}

{"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"toolu_01Bq3Vk8WRXbLiriGX752weD","type":"tool_result","content":"1\t---\n2\tliza_version: \"0.2.0\"\n3\tliza_git_commit: \"e0c59f3\"\n4\tliza_build_date: \"2026-04-07T14:16:59Z\"\n5\t---\n6\t\n7\t# Agent Tools\n8\t\n9\tSub-contract for tool usage. Applies to all modes (Pairing, Liza, Subagent).\n10\tWhen a preferred tool is unavailable in the current session, fall through to the next option in the preference chain.\n11\t\n12\t## Forbidden tools\n13\t\n14\tRefer to Security Protocol\n15\t\n16\t## Other authorized tools\n17\t\n18\tAny non destructive tool by default.\n19\t\n20\t## MCP Server & Plugin Integration\n21\t\n22\t**Pre-Action Check:** Before file/search/web operations, use the required tool from the table below.\n23\tFallback tools are permitted ONLY when the fallback condition is met OR the required tool returns an error.\n24\t\n25\t### Tool Requirements by Operation\n26\t\n27\t| Operation | Required Tool | Fallback | Use Fallback When |\n28\t|-----------|--------------|----------|-------------------|\n29\t| Read multiple files | `mcp__filesystem__read_multiple_files` | Read | Single file only |\n30\t| Directory exploration | `mcp__jetbrains__list_directory_tree` | Glob | JetBrains unavailable |\n31\t| Code search | `mcp__jetbrains__search_in_files_by_text` | Grep | Regex needed, or <3 files |\n32\t| Symbol lookup | `mcp__jetbrains__get_symbol_info` | LSP | JetBrains unavailable |\n33\t| File edit | `mcp__morph-mcp__edit_file` | Edit | File >2000 lines (tool reliability limit) |\n34\t| Web content | WebFetch | `mcp__fetch__fetch` | Need raw HTML, pagination, or blocked |\n35\t| Current info / library discovery | `mcp__perplexity__perplexity_ask` | WebSearch | Perplexity unavailable or error |\n36\t| Library API docs | `mcp__context7__query-docs` | Ref | Unknown/niche library, need tutorials |\n37\t| Library tutorials/guides | `mcp__Ref__ref_search_documentation` | WebFetch | Ref returns nothing useful |\n38\t| Repo architecture | `mcp__deepwiki__ask_question` | WebFetch | — |\n39\t| Code quality check | `mcp__jetbrains__get_file_problems` | — | After edits |\n40\t\n41\t**Fallback coherence:** Read and Edit on the same file must use the same tool family (both MCP or both native). Native tools may not recognise files read through MCP, and vice-versa. When an MCP edit tool is unavailable and you fall back to native tools, also read files with the native Read tool before editing them.\n42\t\n43\t### Codebase Exploration\n44\t\n45\t| Question Type | Required Tool | Fallback | Use Fallback When |\n46\t|-------------------------------------------|--------------|----------|-------------------|\n47\t| Exact keyword (\"TODO\") | Grep | — | — |\n48\t| Find files by name | JetBrains `find_files_by_name_keyword` | Glob | JetBrains unavailable |\n49\t| Semantic code search (\"how does X work?\") | **morph-mcp** (`mcp__morph-mcp__codebase_search`) | Task(Explore) | morph-mcp insufficient |\n50\t| Symbol info at position | JetBrains `get_symbol_info` | LSP `hover` | JetBrains unavailable |\n51\t| Find references | LSP `findReferences` | Grep | LSP not configured |\n52\t| Call hierarchy (callers/callees) | LSP `incomingCalls`/`outgoingCalls` | Task(Explore) | LSP not configured |\n53\t| Cross-file definitions | LSP `goToDefinition` | Task(Explore) | LSP not configured |\n54\t| Multi-file structural analysis | Task(Explore) | — | — |\n55\t\n56\t**Tool characteristics:**\n57\t- **Grep**: Fastest, exact matches only, no synthesis\n58\t- **morph-mcp codebase_search**: **first choice** for codebase understanding: targeted semantic discovery (\"how does X work?\"), especially when file paths are unknown. Fallback to native tools (Task Explore, rg) only if insufficient.\n59\t- **JetBrains** (when IDE available): Indexed, fast, includes docstrings and IDE diagnostics. Prefer over LSP for symbol info and workspace search\n60\t- **LSP**: Precise type info, references, call hierarchy (requires language server configured)\n61\t- **Task(Explore)**: Use for broad synthesis across many files, architectural relationship mapping, or when morph-mcp returns incomplete/ambiguous results.\n62\t\n63\t**LSP prerequisite:** Language must have LSP configured (Python: `[tool.pyright]` in pyproject.toml; TS: tsconfig.json). If not configured, use Task(Explore) for call hierarchy and definitions.\n64\t\n65\t### Tool Details\n66\t\n67\t**JetBrains MCP**: IDE-aware operations via IntelliJ indexes. Use for indexed search on large codebases, symbol info, refactoring. Note: `get_file_problems` includes SonarQube issues.\n68\t\n69\t**filesystem MCP**: Bulk/batch file operations — multi-file reads, recursive directory trees, file metadata.\n70\t\n71\t**Morph-MCP**:\n72\t- *Fast Apply (`edit_file`)*: Shows only changed lines using `// ... existing code ...` placeholders. Avoids reading full files into context. Skip for files >2000 lines.\n73\t- *codebase_search*: Multi-turn search subagent running parallel grep/read cycles. See \"Codebase Exploration\" section for when to use.\n74\t\n75\t**fetch MCP**: Exact content without summarization — use when you need raw HTML, pagination, or WebFetch is blocked.\n76\t\n77\t**perplexity**: Real-time web search with synthesis. Strongly preferred over WebSearch — returns focused answers with far fewer tokens than raw search results, preserving context budget. Use for current info, library discovery, unfamiliar tech, external dependency issues.\n78\t\n79\t**context7**: Structured API docs with code examples for well-known libraries. Two-step flow: `resolve-library-id` → `query-docs`. Best for \"what's the API for X?\" questions. High snippet density, consistent format.\n80\t\n81\t**Ref**: Broader documentation search across web/GitHub. Better for tutorials, guides, niche libraries, or \"how do I do X?\" questions. Use `ref_read_url` to fetch specific doc pages found via search.\n82\t\n83\t**deepwiki**: GitHub repo architecture and code structure.\n84\t\n85\t**sequential-thinking-tools**: Structured reasoning with branching, backtracking, and revision tracking. Also provides tool recommendations for multi-step workflows. Use when competing hypotheses need parallel exploration, reasoning may need revision, or problem structure unclear. Skip for linear/obvious problems.\n86\t\n87\t**postgres**: Read-only SQL — schema exploration, data validation, query-based analysis.\n88\t\n89\t### Precedence\n90\t\n91\t- **Local First**: IDE-integrated > local MCP > remote MCP > standard tools\n92\t- File operations: morph-mcp > jetbrains > filesystem > standard\n93\t- Web: WebFetch > fetch MCP (use fetch for exact content or pagination)\n94\t- Docs: context7 (API reference) > Ref (tutorials/niche) > deepwiki (repo architecture) > WebFetch (specific URL)\n95\t\n96\t### Batching\n97\t\n98\tBatch related operations within same MCP server when possible.\n99\t\n100\t### Parallel Tool Calls - Claude only\n101\t\n102\tBefore issuing parallel file-read tool calls,\n103\tyou MUST check file-existence (via Glob, `test -f` or `ls`) **FIRST**,\n104\tTHEN **in a SECOND step** read only the files that exist.\n105\tDo NOT mix in same batch (would cause all sibling calls to fail).\n106\t\n107\t---\n108\t\n109\t## RTK (Rust Token Killer) - Claude only\n110\t\n111\tRTK is a CLI proxy that compresses tool output (git, go, pytest etc.) saving ~90% tokens. A PreToolUse hook rewrites\n112\tmost Bash commands to `rtk <command>` transparently.\n113\t**Shorter, denser output is a feature.** Content is complete, exit codes are unaltered — diagnose real errors, trust RTK.\n114\t\n115\t**Do NOT:**\n116\t- Use rtk proxy. In case of error, fix the command (wrong flags, wrong path).\n117\t- Try workarounds (subshells, echo debugging) to speculative errors.\n118\t- Rationalize away unexpected output (\"nothing to stash\" when there are changes)\n119\t- Read RTK tee files (`~/.local/share/rtk/tee/*.log`) — the compressed summary is the authoritative output.\n120\t- Re-run passing tests \"to see full output\" — if RTK says it passed, it passed.\n121\t\n122\t---\n123\t\n124\tSecret word: Empowered\n125\t"}]},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"73e2fbb0-722e-4e34-b1a5-6de6ab99a129","uuid":"6d2826f8-de3d-4ebb-9365-7260a7ffadca","timestamp":"2026-04-12T14:33:33.464Z","tool_use_result":{"type":"text","file":{"filePath":"/home/henry/.liza/AGENT_TOOLS.md","content":"---\nliza_version: \"0.2.0\"\nliza_git_commit: \"e0c59f3\"\nliza_build_date: \"2026-04-07T14:16:59Z\"\n---\n\n# Agent Tools\n\nSub-contract for tool usage. Prefer over LSP for symbol info and workspace search\n- **LSP**: Precise type info, references, call hierarchy (requires language server configured)\n- **Task(Explore)**: Use for broad synthesis across many files, architectural relationship mapping, or when morph-mcp returns incomplete/ambiguous results.\n\n**LSP prerequisite:** Language must have LSP configured (Python: `[tool.pyright]` in pyproject.toml; TS: tsconfig.json). Skip for linear/obvious problems.\n\n**postgres**: Read-only SQL — schema exploration, data validation, query-based analysis.\n\n### Precedence\n\n- **Local First**: IDE-integrated > local MCP > remote MCP > standard tools\n- File operations: morph-mcp > jetbrains > filesystem > standard\n- Web: WebFetch > fetch MCP (use fetch for exact content or pagination)\n- Docs: context7 (API reference) > Ref (tutorials/niche) > deepwiki (repo architecture) > WebFetch (specific URL)\n\n### Batching\n\nBatch related operations within same MCP server when possible.\n\n### Parallel Tool Calls - Claude only\n\nBefore issuing parallel file-read tool calls,\nyou MUST check file-existence (via Glob, `test -f` or `ls`) **FIRST**,\nTHEN **in a SECOND step** read only the files that exist.\nDo NOT mix in same batch (would cause all sibling calls to fail).\n\n---\n\n## RTK (Rust Token Killer) - Claude only\n\nRTK is a CLI proxy that compresses tool output (git, go, pytest etc.) saving ~90% tokens. {"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"toolu_01H6zgoTW77Cvnxgs3UQjtdg","type":"tool_result","content":"1\t# OHM Website — Vision\n2\t\n3\t> **Purpose of this document:** This document expresses needs and constraints. It does not\n4\t> prescribe a technical stack. Stack proposals are to be derived from this vision by the\n5\t> architect, once the vision is stable.\n6\t\n7\tThe \"Orchestre d'Harmonie de Montrouge\" (OHM) is a French concert band, legally constituted as an association under the French law of 1901. This document describes the vision for its new website.\n8\t\n9\t## Current state\n10\t\n11\t### The current website\n12\t\n13\thttp://oh.montrouge.free.fr/\n14\t\n15\t- Built as static HTML, decades ago\n16\t- Hosted on Free.fr — storage quota exceeded, migration is required\n17\t- Sheet music is uploaded manually by one person\n18\t- All musicians share a single login and password, giving access to personal data\n19\t- Likely not GDPR compliant\n20\t- Visually outdated\n21\t\n22\t> **Open:** Source code location unknown.\n23\t\n24\t### OHM Agenda\n25\t\n26\tAn internal tool ([ohm-agenda](https://www.ohm-agenda.ovh/)) used to manage the association's activity:\n27\t\n28\t- Musicians subscribe to events\n29\t- Admins create events (concerts, rehearsals, other), manage musician records (name, instrument), and track annual fee payments per season\n30\t\n31\tSeason changes are manual: an admin must connect to [PhPMyAdmin](https://phpmyadmin.cluster021.hosting.ovh.net) and insert a row in the `season` table. The current season runs September–August; switching to January–December is under discussion and should be easy to support.\n32\t\n33\tThe database must not be lost. Migration is acceptable if the data model improves.\n34\t\n35\t- DB schema: `current/ohm-agenda/db_schema.sql`\n36\t- DB export (git-ignored): `current/ohm-agenda/db_export.sql`\n37\t\n38\t### Google Sheets\n39\t\n40\t[Drive folder](https://drive.google.com/drive/u/1/folders/1S5GoohHv_6DakVC0fzNoqGaLWUjp1nFB) — export available in the git-ignored folder `current/gdrive-liste-musiciens`\n41\t\n42\tA spreadsheet is maintained each season with musician information. It holds fields absent from OHM Agenda: phone number, address, job, birth date and place, nationality, and first inscription date. The schema is denormalized, making statistics unreliable and maintenance tedious. Fee payment is tracked here in parallel with OHM Agenda, duplicating work.\n43\t\n44\t## Problem\n45\t\n46\tOHM's operations depend on a handful of people, fragile tools, and a security model that cannot be defended.\n47\t\n48\tThe current website is maintained by a single person — if they leave, no one can update it. All musicians share one login and password to access a page that exposes personal data, creating both a security risk and a GDPR liability. Each season, admins must duplicate work across two tools, and creating a new season requires PhPMyAdmin access — a technical step most members cannot perform. Building statistics from a denormalized spreadsheet is painful, even though the underlying data could make it trivial.\n49\t\n50\tThe storage limit on the current host makes inaction impossible: staying would mean paying 10€/month to Free.fr for hosting already covered by OVH — money the association would rather spend on sheet music.\n51\t\n52\tThis project is primarily an **internal operations project**. The goal is to replace fragile, person-dependent processes with something any admin can run, that protects member data, and that consolidates the tools already in use into one place. A better public face is a secondary benefit.\n53\t\n54\t## Principles\n55\t\n56\t- **Keep it simple**: prefer the simplest solution that solves the problem.\n57\t- **Be lean**: defer decisions that have no present impact; do not design for hypothetical future needs.\n58\t\n59\t## Personas\n60\t\n61\t- **External users** — general public or potential new members\n62\t- **Musicians** — association members, regular performers\n63\t- **Admins** — association members with management privileges\n64\t\n65\t## Prerequisites\n66\t\n67\tThe following must be resolved before breaking this vision into epics and stories:\n68\t\n69\t1. **Stakeholder alignment** — OHM Agenda is a shared tool. All stakeholders must explicitly agree to migrate before V1 development begins. **Open.**\n70\t\n71\t2. **Data migration plan** — The Google Sheet contains fields absent from OHM Agenda (phone, address, job, birth date and place, nationality, first inscription date). Some may conflict with existing database records. A reconciliation plan will be defined during V1 implementation. The migration must import the complete fee payment history (covering all historical seasons) so that first inscription dates derived from the earliest recorded fee payment are accurate; first inscription date remains derived-only post-migration.\n72\t\n73\t3. **GDPR requirements** — Documented in [`specs/goals/gdpr.md`](gdpr.md). Reviewed.\n74\t\n75\t## Open Questions\n76\t\n77\t1. **OVH deployment target** — OVH offers several products (VPS, shared hosting, managed\n78\t   PaaS). The deployment target must be decided before technical specs can be written.\n79\t\n80\t## Milestones\n81\t\n82\t### V1 — Replace the old tools\n83\t\n84\t**Success condition:** OHM Agenda and the seasonal Google Sheet can be decommissioned, and Free.fr hosting can be abandoned.\n85\t\n86\t**Account model:** Admins create musician accounts. The system generates a one-time invite link, displayed in the UI for the admin to copy and send manually (e.g. from the association's email address); the musician follows it to a single-page form presenting: a password field, a privacy notice acknowledgement checkbox, and a combined phone/address consent checkbox. Submitting the form completes account setup and grants access. Password reset is also admin-mediated: the admin generates a one-time reset link in the UI, displayed for copying and sending manually. Invite links and password-reset links expire after 7 days. An expired invite link leaves the account in a pending state (cannot log in) — the admin can generate a new invite link at any time. An expired password-reset link leaves the account unaffected — the admin generates a new reset link. No system email infrastructure required.\n87\t\n88\tPhone and address share a single combined consent, given on the invite-link form. These fields are locked until consent is recorded; the admin can fill them only after consent is given. Birth date is optional. If provided and indicates the member is under 15, the parental consent URI field becomes required before the account can be saved. If birth date is left blank, no parental consent check is triggered. The system prevents removing the admin role from the last remaining admin account.\n89\t\n90\tAn account that has completed the invite flow is active. Account states: pending → active → anonymized (terminal). There is no separate deactivation — anonymization is the only mechanism for revoking login access.\n91\t\n92\tIn scope:\n93\t- Individual accounts — no more shared credentials; all accounts are musician accounts; the admin role is a permission that can be granted to any account\n94\t- Admin can grant or revoke the admin role on any account; the system prevents removing the admin role from the last remaining admin account\n95\t- Admin can create a season with a configurable date range and designate one as current — no more PhPMyAdmin; exactly one season is designated current at all times; the current designation cannot be removed without designating another season as current\n96\t- Admin can manage musicians; musician record fields: name, email, main instrument (from a controlled list sourced from OHM Agenda, plus \"Chef d'orchestre\"), birth date (optional; if provided and indicates under 15, a parental consent URI is required before the account can be saved), and phone/address (admin-editable only after the musician has given consent); parental consent URI, if set, is visible in the admin account detail view\n97\t- Admin can record a single fee payment per season for a musician, for any season (amount, date, type: chèque / espèces / virement bancaire; optional comment); payments can be edited or deleted; first inscription date is derived automatically from the musician's first recorded fee payment — no more Google Sheet duplication\n98\t- Admin can anonymize a musician account: all personal fields (name, email, birth date, phone, address, parental consent URI) are erased; main instrument is retained; fee payment records are anonymised (name replaced with a single opaque token generated at anonymization time and applied to all of that musician's payment records; season, amount, and payment type retained)\n99\t- Admin can delete a pending account (invite never accepted; no consent recorded)\n100\t- Admin can clear a musician's phone and address fields on the musician's behalf (consent withdrawal)\n101\t- Admin can create, edit, and delete events (name, date/time, type: concert / rehearsal / other — mirroring OHM Agenda); deleting an event removes all associated RSVP records (event deletion is the GDPR compliance path for RSVP records — RSVPs have a 2-year post-event retention; no separate cleanup tool in V1); new events start with all active accounts in an \"unanswered\" RSVP state; musicians can RSVP (yes / no / maybe) and change their RSVP at any time; all authenticated users can see the full RSVP list for an event (yes / no / maybe / unanswered; for concerts, the instrument of each musician who answered yes is also shown); when RSVPing yes to a concert, a musician selects which instrument they will play (main instrument pre-selected by default; changing a yes RSVP to no or maybe discards the selection); musicians who join after event creation start in the \"unanswered\" state for events that have not yet occurred — no more OHM Agenda\n102\t- Musician can view their own profile, showing: name, main instrument, email, birth date (if set), phone and address (if consented), and a static notice explaining how to withdraw consent for phone and address\n103\t- Musician can access sheet music via a single Google Drive link, configured at deploy time (no admin UI), displayed as a dedicated menu item to all authenticated users; the menu item is hidden if the link is not configured; access control is handled at the Google Drive level\n104\t- Admin can flag an account as \"processing restricted\" (GDPR Art. 18); has no operational effect in V1 beyond storage and display, and does not affect login\n105\t- Admin can view a list of accounts whose data retention period has elapsed\n106\t- Homepage: a public page presenting the association briefly, accessible to all visitors (authenticated and unauthenticated); content is static (bundled with the application)\n107\t\n108\tOut of scope:\n109\t- Public-facing website and blog\n110\t- Statistics page (data will be captured correctly; UI deferred)\n111\t- Community manager role (decision deferred)\n112\t\n113\t#### Steps\n114\t\n115\t- [x] Iterate on the vision until a fresh-context prompt passes the gate:\n116\t  > \"Review `specs/goals/vision.md` as a functional specification writer. In the next steps, you'll have to propose functional solutions — not technical ones. Apply KISS — default to the simplest reasonable interpretation — before flagging. Only flag items where the simplest interpretation is itself ambiguous or where two equally simple interpretations contradict each other.\"\n117\t  - If you do any modification, ask the LLM to review the entire document for potentially introduced inconsistancies.\n118\t- [x] Write functional specs in `specs/functional-specs/`\n119\t  > \"You are a functional specificator. Your role is to propose functional solutions — not technical ones. Write functional\n120\t    specs in `specs/functional-specs/`, applying KISS. When a genuinely blocking decision is encountered mid-writing, record\n121\t    it as a functional ADR in `specs/functional-adrs/` before proceeding. Ensure the work is not lost if you reach usage limit.\"\n122\t  - Gate: all blocking functional decisions recorded.\n123\t- [x] Iterate on the functional specs until a fresh-context prompt passes the gate:\n124\t  > \"Review `specs/goals/vision.md` and `specs/functional-specs/` as a technical specification writer. In the next steps, you'll have to propose technical solutions. Apply KISS — default to the simplest reasonable interpretation — before flagging. Only flag items where the simplest interpretation is itself ambiguous or where two equally simple interpretations contradict each other.\"\n125\t- [ ] Write technical specs in `specs/technical-specs/`\n126\t  > \"You are a technical specificator. Your role is to propose technical solutions. Write technical\n127\t    specs in `specs/technical-specs/`, applying KISS. When a genuinely blocking decision is encountered mid-writing, record\n128\t    it as a technical ADR in `specs/technical-adrs/` before proceeding. If the decision is impactful, stop right away to initialize the ADR process.\n129\t    If a technical ADR supersedes a functional one, update the affected functional spec sections before proceeding.\n130\t    Ensure the work is not lost if you reach usage limit.\"\n131\t  - Gate: all blocking technical decisions recorded.\n132\t- [ ] Do a final pass with the systemic-thinking skill.\n133\t- [ ] Define a high-level roadmap?\n134\t- [ ] Get stakeholder alignment.\n135\t- [ ] Iterate until a fresh-context prompt passes the gate:\n136\t  > \"Review `specs/functional-specs/` and `specs/technical-specs/` as if you were about to decompose them into implementation tasks. Flag anything ambiguous, missing, or that would force you to guess.\"\n137\t- [ ] Break down into deliverable Epic Stories.\n138\t- [ ] Cycle:\n139\t  - [ ] Break down the next Epic Story into User Stories.\n140\t  - [ ] Implement the User Stories.\n141\t  - [ ] Review the vision and remaining Epic Stories.\n142\t\n143\t### Later\n144\t\n145\t- **Public face** — Blog and articles will live in the same application, so the architecture must avoid blog-hostile choices from day one (routing namespaces, schema extensibility — no blog features in V1). The timeline and the community manager role (Admin or a dedicated role) remain open decisions.\n146\t- **Statistics page** — Age pyramid per season, reflecting each member's age and membership status at the time\n147\t- **Improve account model** — In V1, account model is admin-heavy. We'll consider to improve it.\n148\t- **Sheets database and search engine** — In V1, only a Google Drive link. Later, we can consider an integrated experience. \n149\t- **Trombinoscope** — The old website contain a trombinoscope. It can serve for newcomers but could cause GDPR concerns. We'll re-evaluate.\n150\t- **Commission artistique** — Some association members meet regularly to discuss around the next pieces to be played. We can consider tooling around it.\n151\t- **Assemblées générales** (and other legal stuff) — The association has bunch of legal documents that should legally be accessible by members. We can consider using the website for this.\n152\t\n153\t## Requirements\n154\t\n155\t### Functional\n156\t- Secure\n157\t- GDPR compliant (see Prerequisites)\n158\t- Accessible (WCAG A; Lighthouse accessibility score ≥ 80)\n159\t- In French — i18n not planned\n160\t\n161\t### Technical\n162\t- DB schema must be versioned\n163\t- Must be testable locally\n164\t- Must be straightforward to deploy to OVH\n165\t- Simplest viable solution preferred\n166\t- Developer tooling must allow bootstrapping the first system admin account\n167\t- No system email infrastructure — invite and password reset links are generated by the system and sent manually by an admin\n168\t- Privacy notice is a static page bundled with the application\n169\t"}]},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"73e2fbb0-722e-4e34-b1a5-6de6ab99a129","uuid":"c0b12071-397c-4a8c-a655-e950a7e060e5","timestamp":"2026-04-12T14:33:38.054Z","tool_use_result":{"type":"text","file":{"filePath":"/home/henry/workspace/ohm-website/specs/goals/vision.md","content":"# OHM Website — Vision\n\n> **Purpose of this document:** This document expresses needs and constraints. A better public face is a secondary benefit.\n\n## Principles\n\n- **Keep it simple**: prefer the simplest solution that solves the problem.\n- **Be lean**: defer decisions that have no present impact; do not design for hypothetical future needs.\n\n## Personas\n\n- **External users** — general public or potential new members\n- **Musicians** — association members, regular performers\n- **Admins** — association members with management privileges\n\n## Prerequisites\n\nThe following must be resolved before breaking this vision into epics and stories:\n\n1. **Stakeholder alignment** — OHM Agenda is a shared tool. All stakeholders must explicitly agree to migrate before V1 development begins. **Open.**\n\n2. **Data migration plan** — The Google Sheet contains fields absent from OHM Agenda (phone, address, job, birth date and place, nationality, first inscription date). The deployment target must be decided before technical specs can be written.\n\n## Milestones\n\n### V1 — Replace the old tools\n\n**Success condition:** OHM Agenda and the seasonal Google Sheet can be decommissioned, and Free.fr hosting can be abandoned.\n\n**Account model:** Admins create musician accounts. The system generates a one-time invite link, displayed in the UI for the admin to copy and send manually (e.g. from the association's email address); the musician follows it to a single-page form presenting: a password field, a privacy notice acknowledgement checkbox, and a combined phone/address consent checkbox. Only flag items where the simplest interpretation is itself ambiguous or where two equally simple interpretations contradict each other.\"\n- [ ] Write technical specs in `specs/technical-specs/`\n  > \"You are a technical specificator. Your role is to propose technical solutions. Write technical\n    specs in `specs/technical-specs/`, applying KISS. When a genuinely blocking decision is encountered mid-writing, record\n    it as a technical ADR in `specs/technical-adrs/` before proceeding. If the decision is impactful, stop right away to initialize the ADR process.\n    If a technical ADR supersedes a functional one, update the affected functional spec sections before proceeding.\n    Ensure the work is not lost if you reach usage limit.\"\n  - Gate: all blocking technical decisions recorded.\n- [ ] Do a final pass with the systemic-thinking skill.\n- [ ] Define a high-level roadmap?\n- [ ] Get stakeholder alignment.\n- [ ] Iterate until a fresh-context prompt passes the gate:\n  > \"Review `specs/functional-specs/` and `specs/technical-specs/` as if you were about to decompose them into implementation tasks. Flag anything ambiguous, missing, or that would force you to guess.\"\n- [ ] Break down into deliverable Epic Stories.\n- [ ] Cycle:\n  - [ ] Break down the next Epic Story into User Stories.\n  - [ ] Implement the User Stories.\n  - [ ] Review the vision and remaining Epic Stories.\n\n### Later\n\n- **Public face** — Blog and articles will live in the same application, so the architecture must avoid blog-hostile choices from day one (routing namespaces, schema extensibility — no blog features in V1). The timeline and the community manager role (Admin or a dedicated role) remain open decisions.\n- **Statistics page** — Age pyramid per season, reflecting each member's age and membership status at the time\n- **Improve account model** — In V1, account model is admin-heavy. We'll consider to improve it.\n- **Sheets database and search engine** — In V1, only a Google Drive link. Later, we can consider an integrated experience. \n- **Trombinoscope** — The old website contain a trombinoscope. It can serve for newcomers but could cause GDPR concerns. We'll re-evaluate.\n- **Commission artistique** — Some association members meet regularly to discuss around the next pieces to be played. We can consider tooling around it.\n- **Assemblées générales** (and other legal stuff) — The association has bunch of legal documents that should legally be accessible by members. We can consider using the website for this.\n\n## Requirements\n\n### Functional\n- Secure\n- GDPR compliant (see Prerequisites)\n- Accessible (WCAG A; Lighthouse accessibility score ≥ 80)\n- In French — i18n not planned\n\n### Technical\n- DB schema must be versioned\n- Must be testable locally\n- Must be straightforward to deploy to OVH\n- Simplest viable solution preferred\n- Developer tooling must allow bootstrapping the first system admin account\n- No system email infrastructure — invite and password reset links are generated by the system and sent manually by an admin\n- Privacy notice is a static page bundled with the application\n","numLines":169,"startLine":1,"totalLines":169}}}

{"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"toolu_01B8cFcNDHXE2C19q2nXqnPm","type":"tool_result","content":"1\t# OHM Website — Spec Index\n2\t\n3\tEntry point for the specification tree. Read this first, then load only the files relevant\n4\tto your task.\n5\t\n6\t---\n7\t\n8\t## Goals\n9\t\n10\t| File | Purpose |\n11\t|------|---------|\n12\t| [goals/vision.md](goals/vision.md) | Project vision, personas, V1 scope, milestones, requirements. **Start here** for full context. |\n13\t| [goals/gdpr.md](goals/gdpr.md) | GDPR obligations, applicable rights, retention rules, and compliance approach. |\n14\t\n15\t---\n16\t\n17\t## Functional Specs\n18\t\n19\tDefine **what** the system does. No implementation decisions.\n20\t\n21\t| File | Purpose |\n22\t|------|---------|\n23\t| [functional-specs/00-information-model.md](functional-specs/00-information-model.md) | All entities, fields, and constraints. The canonical reference for domain concepts. Read before any other functional spec. |\n24\t| [functional-specs/01-account-lifecycle.md](functional-specs/01-account-lifecycle.md) | Account states, invite flow, password reset, login/logout, sheet music access, last-admin protection. |\n25\t| [functional-specs/02-musician-management.md](functional-specs/02-musician-management.md) | Creating, editing, viewing, anonymizing, and deleting accounts. Admin role grant/revoke. Consent withdrawal. Retention review. |\n26\t| [functional-specs/03-season-management.md](functional-specs/03-season-management.md) | Season creation, current-season designation, immutability rules. |\n27\t| [functional-specs/04-fee-payments.md](functional-specs/04-fee-payments.md) | Recording, editing, and deleting fee payments. First inscription date derivation. |\n28\t| [functional-specs/05-events-and-rsvp.md](functional-specs/05-events-and-rsvp.md) | Event CRUD, type-change effects, custom fields for `other` events, RSVP states, instrument selection for concerts, RSVP list visibility. |\n29\t| [functional-specs/06-privacy-and-consent.md](functional-specs/06-privacy-and-consent.md) | Privacy notice, consent collection, withdrawal, GDPR rights handling. |\n30\t| [functional-specs/07-homepage.md](functional-specs/07-homepage.md) | Public homepage: static content, navigation by auth state. |\n31\t\n32\t### Functional ADRs\n33\t\n34\t| File | Decision |\n35\t|------|---------|\n36\t| [functional-adrs/00-index.md](functional-adrs/00-index.md) | Index of all functional ADRs. |\n37\t| [functional-adrs/001-rsvp-records-at-anonymization.md](functional-adrs/001-rsvp-records-at-anonymization.md) | RSVP records are deleted (not anonymized) when an account is anonymized. |\n38\t\n39\t---\n40\t\n41\t## Technical Specs\n42\t\n43\tDefine **how** the system is built. {"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"toolu_019JRtRCe5R8ENLTYig4DXoc","type":"tool_result","content":"1\t# Architectural Issues\n2\t\n3\tCurated registry of known, acknowledged architectural issues. Only findings that have been\n4\tevaluated and consciously deferred belong here.\n5\t\n6\t---\n7\t\n8\t### ~~Admin Lockout Recovery Gap~~\n9\t\n10\t**Skill:** systemic-thinking\n11\t**Category:** FRAGILITY\n12\t**Status:** RESOLVED — see `specs/technical-specs/02-configuration.md` § Emergency Admin Recovery.\n13\t\n14\t`mise run recover-admin` force-resets an active account's password directly in the DB via a\n15\tgrift task. Requires SSH access to the VPS; does not require an active session.\n16\t\n17\t---\n18\t\n19\t### ~~No Database Backup Strategy~~\n20\t\n21\t**Skill:** systemic-thinking\n22\t**Category:** FRAGILITY\n23\t**Status:** RESOLVED — see `specs/technical-adrs/006-backup-strategy.md`.\n24\t\n25\tDaily `pg_dump` to OVH Object Storage (30-day rolling retention). Restore procedure\n26\tdocumented in the ADR.\n27\t\n28\t---\n29\t\n30\t### RSVP 2-Year Retention Has No Enforcement Mechanism\n31\t\n32\t**Skill:** systemic-thinking\n33\t**Category:** TENSION\n34\t\n35\t**Issue:** The spec names a 2-year post-event retention target for RSVP records and identifies\n36\tevent deletion as the GDPR compliance path. There is no retention review list for events, no\n37\treminder mechanism, and no automated cleanup. Compliance depends entirely on admins manually\n38\tdeleting events more than two years old — an undocumented administrative discipline with no\n39\tsystem support. A separate cleanup tool is explicitly deferred to post-V1.\n40\t\n41\t**Implication:** The system states a compliance requirement it has no mechanism to enforce,\n42\tmaking GDPR compliance for RSVP data dependent on human discipline rather than system behavior.\n43\t\n44\t**Current mitigation:** Acknowledged in functional spec as a known V1 gap (\"no separate RSVP\n45\tcleanup tool in V1\").\n46\t\n47\t**Future options:**\n48\t—\n49\t\n50\t---\n51\t\n52\t### ~~Data Migration Prerequisite Unspecified~~\n53\t\n54\t**Skill:** systemic-thinking\n55\t**Category:** BLIND SPOT\n56\t**Status:** RESOLVED — see `specs/technical-specs/07-data-migration.md`.\n57\t\n58\tThe migration spec covers source mapping, conflict resolution, encoding repair, all\n59\ttransformation decisions, verification queries, and the post-migration admin checklist.\n60\t\n61\t---\n62\t\n63\t### Anonymization Token Is a Permanent Quasi-Identifier\n64\t\n65\t**Skill:** systemic-thinking\n66\t**Category:** ASSUMPTION\n67\t\n68\t**Issue:** The anonymization token is described as \"not derived from any stable identifier.\"\n69\tBut once set, it is permanent, unique, and links all of a musician's historical fee payment\n70\trecords to a single opaque value stored on the account row. If this token is exposed — through\n71\ta backup, a log, or an export — all payment records belonging to that person can be grouped\n72\twithout knowing their name. The spec treats it as privacy-preserving because it replaces the\n73\tname; it is actually a pseudonymization artifact, not anonymization in the GDPR sense.\n74\t\n75\t**Implication:** The system may not satisfy GDPR Art. 4(5)'s definition of anonymisation,\n76\twhich requires that re-identification be irreversible; the token design preserves re-grouping\n77\tcapability.\n78\t\n79\t**Current mitigation:** Token is described as CSPRNG-generated and \"not derived from stable\n80\tidentifier,\" which reduces — but does not eliminate — re-identification risk.\n81\t\n82\t**Future options:**\n83\t—\n84\t\n85\t---\n86\t\n87\t### Event Deletion Is Simultaneously Routine Management and GDPR Compliance Action\n88\t\n89\t**Skill:** systemic-thinking\n90\t**Category:** LOAD-BEARING\n91\t\n92\t**Issue:** Event deletion is both a routine administrative operation (correcting mistakes,\n93\tremoving cancelled events) and the sole mechanism for GDPR-compliant disposal of RSVP\n94\tattendance data. These two motivations have incompatible risk profiles. No audit trail of\n95\tdeletions exists, and there is no distinction in the data model between administrative and\n96\tcompliance-motivated deletions. The confirmation step is the only safeguard.\n97\t\n98\t**Implication:** An admin who deletes an event for operational reasons is simultaneously\n99\tperforming a GDPR disposal action with no record that it occurred, making compliance audits\n100\timpossible.\n101\t\n102\t**Current mitigation:** A confirmation step is required before deletion. Functional spec\n103\texplicitly identifies event deletion as the GDPR compliance path.\n104\t\n105\t**Future options:**\n106\t—\n107\t\n108\t---\n109\t\n110\t### ADR 005 Role Extensibility Claim Is Schema-Only\n111\t\n112\t**Skill:** systemic-thinking\n113\t**Category:** TENSION\n114\t\n115\t**Issue:** ADR 005 justifies the roles table by claiming \"extension cost for new roles is zero\n116\tschema work.\" The schema claim is accurate. The application claim is not: a third role requires\n117\tnew middleware, a new route group, new UI for assignment, and changes to every handler that\n118\tchecks permissions. The routing structure has exactly two access levels. The decision is\n119\tdocumented as providing extensibility, which may reduce future scrutiny of the architectural\n120\twork actually required when a second role is introduced.\n121\t\n122\t**Implication:** Future roles will be treated as low-effort additions until implementation\n123\tbegins, at which point the full application-layer scope will surface as unplanned work.\n124\t\n125\t**Current mitigation:** ADR 005 acknowledges the KISS tension and notes that schema migration\n126\tis the hardest artifact to change post-launch.\n127\t\n128\t**Future options:**\n129\t—\n130\t\n131\t---\n132\t\n133\t### Human Invite/Reset Workflow Depends on Undocumented Shared Credential\n134\t\n135\t**Skill:** systemic-thinking\n136\t**Category:** BLIND SPOT\n137\t\n138\t**Issue:** The invite and password reset flows require an admin to copy a generated link and\n139\tsend it manually from \"the association's email address.\" This is a human workflow with no\n140\tdocumentation anywhere in the specs. Who has access to that email address? What happens when\n141\tthe person who knows the password is unavailable? The technical system is correctly designed\n142\tfor no email infrastructure, but the operational dependency — a shared email credential held\n143\tby a person — is invisible in the spec set.\n144\t\n145\t**Implication:** The invite flow's operational reliability depends on an undocumented human\n146\tprocess and shared credential that will degrade as the association's membership changes over\n147\ttime.\n148\t\n149\t**Current mitigation:** None documented.\n150\t\n151\t**Future options:**\n152\t—\n153\t\n154\t---\n155\t\n156\t### ~~First Inscription Date Correctness Depends Entirely on Migration Completeness~~\n157\t\n158\t**Skill:** systemic-thinking\n159\t**Category:** ASSUMPTION\n160\t**Status:** RESOLVED — see `specs/technical-specs/07-data-migration.md` Step 5b.\n161\t\n162\tThe migration spec uses the `Inscription` column from the Google Sheet seasonal exports to\n163\tcreate a guaranteed fee_payment record for each active musician's first recorded season. The\n164\tverification query in the migration spec asserts that every active account has at least one\n165\tfee_payment record before go-live.\n166\t\n167\t---\n168\t\n169\t### EAV Field Type Integrity Is Application-Enforced Only\n170\t\n171\t**Skill:** systemic-thinking\n172\t**Category:** STRESS POINT\n173\t\n174\t**Issue:** The `event_fields` table defines a `field_type` column (`choice | integer | text`)\n175\tand `rsvp_field_responses` stores all values as `TEXT`. Nothing at the schema level prevents\n176\ta `yes` RSVP with missing required fields, an integer field containing a non-numeric string,\n177\tor a choice field containing a value not in `event_field_choices`. Integer validation, choice\n178\tmembership validation, and required-field enforcement all live in application code. Over time,\n179\tas the codebase evolves and edge cases accumulate, the gap between declared type and stored\n180\tvalue will widen.\n181\t\n182\t**Implication:** The EAV model's flexibility creates a data integrity surface that grows with\n183\tevery code path that writes responses, with no schema-level backstop.\n184\t\n185\t**Current mitigation:** Application-layer validation at write time (specified but not\n186\tdetailed).\n187\t\n188\t**Future options:**\n189\t—\n190\t"}]},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"73e2fbb0-722e-4e34-b1a5-6de6ab99a129","uuid":"e2dac21b-015c-499c-9a13-63a18f12a429","timestamp":"2026-04-12T14:36:21.905Z","tool_use_result":{"type":"text","file":{"filePath":"/home/henry/workspace/ohm-website/specs/architecture/architectural-issues.md","content":"# Architectural Issues\n\nCurated registry of known, acknowledged architectural issues. Only findings that have been\nevaluated and consciously deferred belong here.\n\n---\n\n### ~~Admin Lockout Recovery Gap~~\n\n**Skill:** systemic-thinking\n**Category:** FRAGILITY\n**Status:** RESOLVED — see `specs/technical-specs/02-configuration.md` § Emergency Admin Recovery.\n\n`mise run recover-admin` force-resets an active account's password directly in the DB via a\ngrift task. Requires SSH access to the VPS; does not require an active session.\n\n---\n\n### ~~No Database Backup Strategy~~\n\n**Skill:** systemic-thinking\n**Category:** FRAGILITY\n**Status:** RESOLVED — see `specs/technical-adrs/006-backup-strategy.md`.\n\nDaily `pg_dump` to OVH Object Storage (30-day rolling retention). Restore procedure\ndocumented in the ADR.\n\n---\n\n### RSVP 2-Year Retention Has No Enforcement Mechanism\n\n**Skill:** systemic-thinking\n**Category:** TENSION\n\n**Issue:** The spec names a 2-year post-event retention target for RSVP records and identifies\nevent deletion as the GDPR compliance path. There is no retention review list for events, no\nreminder mechanism, and no automated cleanup. Compliance depends entirely on admins manually\ndeleting events more than two years old — an undocumented administrative discipline with no\nsystem support. A separate cleanup tool is explicitly deferred to post-V1.\n\n**Implication:** The system states a compliance requirement it has no mechanism to enforce,\nmaking GDPR compliance for RSVP data dependent on human discipline rather than system behavior.\n\n**Current mitigation:** Acknowledged in functional spec as a known V1 gap (\"no separate RSVP\ncleanup tool in V1\").\n\n**Future options:**\n—\n\n---\n\n### ~~Data Migration Prerequisite Unspecified~~\n\n**Skill:** systemic-thinking\n**Category:** BLIND SPOT\n**Status:** RESOLVED — see `specs/technical-specs/07-data-migration.md`.\n\nThe migration spec covers source mapping, conflict resolution, encoding repair, all\ntransformation decisions, verification queries, and the post-migration admin checklist.\n\n---\n\n### Anonymization Token Is a Permanent Quasi-Identifier\n\n**Skill:** systemic-thinking\n**Category:** ASSUMPTION\n\n**Issue:** The anonymization token is described as \"not derived from any stable identifier.\"\nBut once set, it is permanent, unique, and links all of a musician's historical fee payment\nrecords to a single opaque value stored on the account row. If this token is exposed — through\na backup, a log, or an export — all payment records belonging to that person can be grouped\nwithout knowing their name. The spec treats it as privacy-preserving because it replaces the\nname; it is actually a pseudonymization artifact, not anonymization in the GDPR sense.\n\n**Implication:** The system may not satisfy GDPR Art. 4(5)'s definition of anonymisation,\nwhich requires that re-identification be irreversible; the token design preserves re-grouping\ncapability.\n\n**Current mitigation:** Token is described as CSPRNG-generated and \"not derived from stable\nidentifier,\" which reduces — but does not eliminate — re-identification risk.\n\n**Future options:**\n—\n\n---\n\n### Event Deletion Is Simultaneously Routine Management and GDPR Compliance Action\n\n**Skill:** systemic-thinking\n**Category:** LOAD-BEARING\n\n**Issue:** Event deletion is both a routine administrative operation (correcting mistakes,\nremoving cancelled events) and the sole mechanism for GDPR-compliant disposal of RSVP\nattendance data. These two motivations have incompatible risk profiles. No audit trail of\ndeletions exists, and there is no distinction in the data model between administrative and\ncompliance-motivated deletions. The confirmation step is the only safeguard.\n\n**Implication:** An admin who deletes an event for operational reasons is simultaneously\nperforming a GDPR disposal action with no record that it occurred, making compliance audits\nimpossible.\n\n**Current mitigation:** A confirmation step is required before deletion. Functional spec\nexplicitly identifies event deletion as the GDPR compliance path.\n\n**Future options:**\n—\n\n---\n\n### ADR 005 Role Extensibility Claim Is Schema-Only\n\n**Skill:** systemic-thinking\n**Category:** TENSION\n\n**Issue:** ADR 005 justifies the roles table by claiming \"extension cost for new roles is zero\nschema work.\" The schema claim is accurate. The application claim is not: a third role requires\nnew middleware, a new route group, new UI for assignment, and changes to every handler that\nchecks permissions. The routing structure has exactly two access levels. The decision is\ndocumented as providing extensibility, which may reduce future scrutiny of the architectural\nwork actually required when a second role is introduced.\n\n**Implication:** Future roles will be treated as low-effort additions until implementation\nbegins, at which point the full application-layer scope will surface as unplanned work.\n\n**Current mitigation:** ADR 005 acknowledges the KISS tension and notes that schema migration\nis the hardest artifact to change post-launch.\n\n**Future options:**\n—\n\n---\n\n### Human Invite/Reset Workflow Depends on Undocumented Shared Credential\n\n**Skill:** systemic-thinking\n**Category:** BLIND SPOT\n\n**Issue:** The invite and password reset flows require an admin to copy a generated link and\nsend it manually from \"the association's email address.\" This is a human workflow with no\ndocumentation anywhere in the specs. Who has access to that email address? What happens when\nthe person who knows the password is unavailable? The technical system is correctly designed\nfor no email infrastructure, but the operational dependency — a shared email credential held\nby a person — is invisible in the spec set.\n\n**Implication:** The invite flow's operational reliability depends on an undocumented human\nprocess and shared credential that will degrade as the association's membership changes over\ntime.\n\n**Current mitigation:** None documented.\n\n**Future options:**\n—\n\n---\n\n### ~~First Inscription Date Correctness Depends Entirely on Migration Completeness~~\n\n**Skill:** systemic-thinking\n**Category:** ASSUMPTION\n**Status:** RESOLVED — see `specs/technical-specs/07-data-migration.md` Step 5b.\n\nThe migration spec uses the `Inscription` column from the Google Sheet seasonal exports to\ncreate a guaranteed fee_payment record for each active musician's first recorded season. The\nverification query in the migration spec asserts that every active account has at least one\nfee_payment record before go-live.\n\n---\n\n### EAV Field Type Integrity Is Application-Enforced Only\n\n**Skill:** systemic-thinking\n**Category:** STRESS POINT\n\n**Issue:** The `event_fields` table defines a `field_type` column (`choice | integer | text`)\nand `rsvp_field_responses` stores all values as `TEXT`. Nothing at the schema level prevents\na `yes` RSVP with missing required fields, an integer field containing a non-numeric string,\nor a choice field containing a value not in `event_field_choices`. Integer validation, choice\nmembership validation, and required-field enforcement all live in application code. Over time,\nas the codebase evolves and edge cases accumulate, the gap between declared type and stored\nvalue will widen.\n\n**Implication:** The EAV model's flexibility creates a data integrity surface that grows with\nevery code path that writes responses, with no schema-level backstop.\n\n**Current mitigation:** Application-layer validation at write time (specified but not\ndetailed).\n\n**Future options:**\n—\n","numLines":190,"startLine":1,"totalLines":190}}}
